items.myth: Regression and classification require different kinds of input features.
items.reality: The same feature vector x can feed either problem type — the distinction is entirely about the target y and the output head/loss, not about whether inputs are numeric or categorical.
items.example: Text embeddings from the same encoder can predict a sentiment class (classification) or a 1–5 star rating as a number (regression).

items.myth: If the model outputs a number, it must be regression.
items.reality: Classifiers often output numbers: probabilities, logits, or scores. Regression means the target y itself is continuous, not that outputs are numeric.
items.example: P(fraud)=0.82 is a classification probability, not a regression forecast of "0.82 units of fraud."

items.myth: Classification is always easier because accuracy is simple.
items.reality: Classification introduces class imbalance, threshold selection, and multi-class calibration challenges. Regression has its own issues (outliers, scale), but neither is trivial.
items.example: 99% accuracy on 1% fraud rate can mean the classifier never flags fraud — accuracy alone misleads.

items.myth: You must pick one problem type and never convert between them.
items.reality: Teams sometimes derive classification from regression (threshold on predicted score) or regression from classification (predict expected value). The canonical framing should still match the primary deployment need.
items.example: Predict expected revenue (regression) then flag "high value" leads (classification) for sales — two views of related signals, different primary metrics.
